textPlain: Ford could afford to purchase dedicated steel-stamping presses to churn out pressed-steel crankcases, which were cheaper and used less material than the cast iron employed by other manufacturersEventually it was even cheaper to not have a separate crankcase and just cast it with the rest of the engine block, as is now common practice (and has been for around a century).

Shoutout to the Henry Ford Museum located in Dearborn, Michigan which is a treasure trove of 19th and early 20th century technology. A true celebration of, what I would consider, the most physically transformative era in human history thus far.
